topic: Decision Making to Improve Financial Performance
subject: business-studies

items.q: Are profit and cash the same thing?
items.correct: No - profit is revenue earned minus costs incurred, while cash is money actually flowing in and out; credit sales create profit on paper before any cash arrives.
items.misconception: Yes - if a business is profitable it must have plenty of cash.
items.random_wrong: Yes - profit is just the accountant's word for the bank balance.
items.why: Timing is the missing idea. A firm can sell profitably on 90-day credit and still be unable to pay this month's wages - why cash flow forecasting exists.

items.q: Does raising the selling price always increase total revenue?
items.correct: No - a higher price usually reduces the quantity demanded; whether revenue rises depends on price elasticity of demand.
items.misconception: Yes - a higher price per unit guarantees more revenue overall.
items.random_wrong: Yes - customers ignore prices when buying.
items.why: Students multiply the new price by the old quantity. Revenue is price times quantity, and quantity reacts to price - elasticity decides which effect wins.

items.q: Do fixed costs fall when a business produces less?
items.correct: No - fixed costs such as rent and salaries stay the same in the short run regardless of output; it is variable costs that change with production.
items.misconception: Yes - all costs shrink proportionally when output drops.
items.random_wrong: Yes - fixed costs disappear if the factory pauses for a week.
items.why: The word 'fixed' is about output-independence, not permanence. This is why falling sales are dangerous: revenue drops while fixed costs march on.

items.correct: Fixed costs divided by contribution per unit
items.why: It's Fixed costs divided by contribution per unit. “Revenue divided by total costs” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: The actual result is worse than the budgeted figure
items.misconception: The actual result exactly matches the budget
items.random_wrong: The budget is set too low deliberately
items.why: Correct answer: The actual result is worse than the budgeted figure. “The actual result exactly matches the budget” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A profitable business can still fail if it:
items.correct: Runs out of cash to pay bills when they are due
items.why: It's Runs out of cash to pay bills when they are due. “Has too many customers” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: The amount by which current output exceeds break-even output
items.misconception: The profit made on each unit
items.random_wrong: The cash held in the safe
items.why: It's The amount by which current output exceeds break-even output. “The profit made on each unit” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A firm has fixed costs of £20,000. Its product sells for £10 with variable costs of £6 per unit. What is the break-even output?
items.why: The real figure is 5000 units. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
